narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam based on academic and personal profiles like yours. The model estimated an 88.2% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is why your application was rejected—law schools use this to prioritize candidates with stronger predicted odds of passing and practicing successfully. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year, far below the typical student's midpoint around the 5th or 6th decile. This low performance signal stood out as the strongest driver, suggesting to the model that your later academic trajectory raises serious concerns about bar exam readiness. Next in influence was your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which actually worked in your favor by exceeding the average of 3.21 and pulling the prediction slightly toward passing, showing your college record as a relative strength. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also below the average of about 5.7, contributed further toward the failure prediction, reinforcing a pattern of early underperformance compared to most applicants. While your LSAT score of 30 is below the typical 36.7 and your full-time enrollment plans align with the vast majority at 92.7%, these played lesser roles here. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having solid undergrad grades but weaker high school deciles that overshadowed them, tipping the scales against you. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a person or lawyer—it's the model highlighting patterns from past students where similar early struggles correlated with bar challenges. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to average, strengthening your high school record explanations in a reapplication, or targeting schools with more holistic reviews that weigh your GPA positively. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early or gain relevant experience to demonstrate growth. With focused improvements, a stronger application next cycle could shift this prediction in your favor. Keep pushing—you've already shown college-level capability that many lack.
